result:
The first thing I notice when I open the window is the way the air piles in like alphabet soup, vowels first, steam-heavy and salted. Downstairs someone is learning the piano, a stubborn arpeggio that climbs three steps and slides back two, over and over, as if the hands keep forgetting the next floor number. The sound slips through walls the color of old toast and finds my room like a polite burglar: it takes nothing but rearranges the furniture in my head. I sit on the sill and let it.

Across the alley a woman is hanging socks the way sailors hang flags on a windless day—methodically, as though each pair is a declaration. One red stripe to port, one white stripe to starboard. I count the ships until she notices me noticing and the curtain of my consideration half-drawn. She lifts her chin in the small salute neighbors give each other when language would feel theatrical. Then she moves indoors and the alley falls back into its usual shade of unsent letter.

At my feet a loose floorboard has curled upward like a lip. I got drunk last weekend and pried it open, hunting for the ghost of whatever I used to hide there—cigarettes, maybe, or ransom notes to myself. Instead I found a Polaroid I don’t remember: my mother at twenty-five, leaning on the hood of a Plymouth the color of drowned nickels, her knees sun-browned and her smile urgent, as if the camera had only a second before she sprinted off to whatever music was playing inside her. I stared at it so long I began to hear static, the white noise before a secret is spoken.

Now the board stays up, a skylight into the past. Little grains of sawdust keep appearing around it like sand after returning from a beach holiday. Every morning I sweep them into a jar, the kind fancy salts come in when people believe in preserving small things. Tonight the jar will be nearly full, its side reading “Mediterranean Coarse” in blue letters. Mediterranean: middle of the earth, middle of the heart. Coarse: what grief becomes when you touch it too often without gloves.

I decide to go out, sliding the picture into my back pocket the way one pockets a dare. The hallway smells of curry and piano polish. On the second landing I meet Mrs. Kelleher carrying a laundry basket roughly the size of a grade-school globe. We orbit each other on the narrow stairs, two faulty moons. She says, “It’s the humidity, darling. Everything swells like teenage boys.” I nod as if the whole day were merely bloated. Her eyes flick to my bare feet and she adds, “You’re leaking daylight.” I’m still translating that when the front door closes behind me.

Outside, the sidewalk is set to simmer. The heat has its own handwriting: l’s that loop, t’s crossed like stitches. Each curb is a faint exhalation from the planet’s dream. I walk until shoelessness gets impractical and sit on a bench already occupied by a sandwich crust and a penny worn smooth as a wish. There’s a church across the street; its bell is groaning eleven times for someone inside who still trusts round numbers. A pigeon wearing one white feather like a private joke lands near my toes. I open my fist; inside is the desire to call a man who once catalogued my flaws as tenderly as if they were bottle caps he collected. The phone stays in my pocket; desire likes to be housed but not addressed.

Instead, I take out the Polaroid. The mosaic light through the maple above tessellates my mother’s face. She looks suddenly借阅的, borrowed the way library books are held carefully because someone’s name is inked on the flyleaf. I carry that name in my spine right above the place sciatica now modulates my weather. A car passes, vibrating with a bass line whose heart might be in Havana or Detroit. It drums the pavement into a soft drum, the rhythm of a body learning to forgive itself.

Behind the church there is a playground where the swings have given up sweeping the sky and hang like executed marionettes. The slide is arched like a whale carcass, too hot to touch. I walk through anyway, barefoot, the metal branding my arches like confirmation. Children have chalked the hopscotch squares crooked, skipped stones drawn as planets inside each. I jump to three (green Neptune), then four (Saturn with a ring like a skipped heartbeat). My landing sends chalk dust up, a private eclipse. Somewhere, very close, a sprinkler hisses prayerfully.

The supermarket plaza next door is selling blueberries under a tent. Six pints for the price of five. I buy nothing, just stand close enough to smell their refrigerated dream of forests. A woman in an apron recites the rules of a raffle: first prize a bicycle, second a year of free coffee, third an issue of National Geographic from 1987 with all the water photos already circled by a previous owner. I fill out a ticket in handwriting my teachers once despaired of, all tilting vowels still fleeing to port. Name: “Next Year’s Rain.” Number: “Unlisted.” I deposit it and feel oddly weightless, as if I’ve submitted a part of the sky.

Back on the route home the afternoon has leaned itself drunkenly against the curb. Clouds rebuild architecture every minute just to abandon it. A boy pedals by on a bike hung with soda cans—tin tambourines announcing his joy. I want to applaud the way one applauds sunsets: silently, mouth open. Pieces of my day rearrange themselves around this image. The piano downstairs skips a beat. My mother in the Polaroid turns her head just enough that the wind might be whispering directions. I feel for the picture in my pocket—it’s warm as fresh bread. Somewhere inside, an arpeggio resolves.

There are rooms within rooms, fields within fields. The city is a book printed on both sides of the leaves and sometimes you read forward, sometimes backward, sometimes only the gutter where the staple holds it together. The staple is your spine, the ache a stipulation. I ascend the stairs noticing that Mrs. Kelleher is now beating rugs against the railing, each thud a saved conversation. She sees my feet and smiles as though she expected them, barefoot prophecy. I hoist my own invisible rug, let it slap the sky to empty it of chalk dust and riots of blueberries and borrowed mothers.

When I open my apartment door the room smells of pine and unopened envelopes. The floorboard mouth is still open, a whispering well. I kneel, take out the Polaroid, and slide it back in. The jar of sawdust glows amber on the sill. I look at the label again—Mediterranean Coarse—and, for the first time, I read “medi-terranean,” the sea in the middle of land, which is to say, the heart wherever you find it. I close the board like sealing a letter I still can’t finish.

Somewhere downstairs the piano finds its missing note and steps onto the next floor.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
